segments.text: Russ: Erm ... hello Professor, I'm Russ.
Russ: I spoke to you on the phone last week.
Tutor: Ah, yes, come in and have a seat.
Tutor: You want to talk to me about the archaeology course, right?
Russ: Yes, I've read the handbook, but I still have some questions and want to know more details before deciding whether to do it.
Tutor: Right.
Tutor: So, what would you like to know?
Russ: Well, first of all, when I choose the archaeology course, can I integrate it with other subjects?
Russ: For example, one in anthropology?
Tutor: Yes, it can be combined with any other subjects apart from classical history.
Tutor: That's because there is some overlap in the lecture times for those two courses.
Tutor: We weren't able to coordinate them.
Russ: That's OK.
Russ: Then about the modules, could you tell me the details?
Tutor: In the first semester, there are three modules, all of which are compulsory.
Russ: What about optional modules?
Tutor: Well, we don't offer optional modules until next semester.
Russ: OK.
Tutor: The first module is called Object Matters, and Dr Morris teaches it.
Tutor: This module is mainly about what can be learnt from specific artifacts such as pottery and stone tools.
Russ: That's something I've never heard about.
Russ: I'm interested.
Russ: How is the module presented?
Russ: I mean, is it all lectures?
Tutor: Well, you mean the learning method.
Tutor: In this case, its lectures are combined with practical sessions, so it's a mixture.
Russ: What about the content of the module?
Russ: I suppose we will be looking at different kinds of archaeological remains and how to date them, that kind of thing.
Tutor: To some extent, yes.
Tutor: But the module is based on processes.

Tutor: And it covers four aspects in detail: first of all, recording material, and then classification, then the interpretation of the data, that's how archaeologists conclude their findings, and finally, display.
Tutor: Is that OK for you?
Russ: I think so.
Russ: Yes, thanks!
Russ: Oh, one important thing, how is the module assessed?
Russ: Should we take an exam at the end of the semester?
Tutor: No.
Tutor: It's all based on coursework after each lecture, so try your best when the lecturer assigns you a task.
Russ: OK.
Tutor: The second module's title is Towns and Cities.
Tutor: It is taught by Professor Elliot, the head of our department.
Tutor: And as the name suggests, the module focuses on the origins of built environments and how they developed.
Russ: That's mainly factual, I suppose.
Tutor: It is.
Tutor: And for that reason, it is assessed by examination.
Tutor: But instead of a written exam, we set it as an oral one, as we think most students prefer this way.
Russ: It sounds a bit scary.
Russ: I prefer written exams.
Tutor: Don't worry too much.
Tutor: Most of our students find they enjoy it.
Russ: OK.
Tutor: And then the third module is called Method and Science.
Tutor: And in this module, Dr Thompson will introduce you to the standard techniques used in archaeological fieldwork and analysis, such as excavating and dating.
Russ: What about the learning method for this module?
Russ: Are there any lectures, or it's all laboratory work?
Tutor: Oh, there aren't any lectures.
Tutor: It combines half lab work and half seminars.
Russ: What about the assessment?
Tutor: Oh, you are lucky; this module has no formal assessment.
Tutor: But right at the end of the module, you will have to take part in a site survey.
Russ: That's great!
Russ: Where shall we go for the site survey?
Tutor: Sorry, I can't tell you the location yet.
Tutor: That would be announced later.
Tutor: The date for that is the week beginning the 10th of March.
Russ: OK.
Russ: That's all I want to know.
Russ: Thank you very much, Professor.
Russ: Bye!
Tutor: You are welcome, Russ.
Tutor: Bye!
